title: Biomimicry: Nature’s R&D Department (and We’re the Interns)
summary: Nature’s been designing for 3.8 billion years, and it’s still ahead of us. From kingfishers to termites, biomimicry shows how geckos, whales, and bugs out-design humans. For learning design, the trick is simple: steal smart — smooth entries, sticky progress, self-regulating systems, and a few bumps to keep it real.
content:
If you think “innovation” means slapping AI on everything, congratulations — you’re already dumber than a slime mould.

Biomimicry is the fancy word for “stealing nature’s homework and pretending it was our idea.” Except nature has been iterating for 3.8 billion years without a single sticky-note workshop or design sprint. (Imagine that — survival without a Miro board.)

Let’s look at how nature routinely out-designs us — and then ask what this means for learning design, where half the time we’re just trying to get students to read the bloody rubric.

### 1. The bullet train that owed its nose to a bird

The Japanese Shinkansen train once created thunderous sonic booms when leaving tunnels. Engineers, clearly desperate, looked at the kingfisher — a bird that dives into water without a splash. Copy the beak, reshape the train’s nose, and voilà: 10% faster, 15% less electricity, and no neighbours filing noise complaints.

👉 **Lesson for learning design:** Maybe stop forcing students into echo-chamber tunnels of text and give them beak-shaped entry points. Clear, clean, splash-free ways into content. Less “thunderous boom” of PDFs. More elegant dives.

### 2. The gecko that laughed at Velcro

Velcro is cute, but geckos walk up walls like it’s a Tuesday. Their secret? Millions of microscopic hairs (setae) on their feet that exploit van der Waals forces (molecular stickiness without glue). Researchers now study “gecko tape” that can hold 300kg but peels off clean.

👉 **Learning design parallel:** Instead of one massive “stick” (the final exam), use thousands of micro-grips — little formative activities, feedback loops, peer nudges. Make the learning journey sticky in small, invisible ways, not with duct-tape assessments at the end.

### 3. Termites: better architects than your facilities team

In Zimbabwe, Eastgate Centre was modelled after termite mounds, which maintain near-constant temperatures by clever airflow. The result: a 90% reduction in conventional air-conditioning use.

👉 **Learning design equivalent:** Stop overheating students with 12 different platforms. Create “passive learning systems” that regulate themselves — intuitive navigation, consistent module layouts, content that breathes. (Sadly, termites are better at scaffolding than most course coordinators.)

### 4. Whale fins and wind power

The bumps (tubercles) on humpback whale fins reduce drag and improve lift. Wind turbine blades modelled on this principle are up to 20% more efficient.

👉 **Lesson:** Build in turbulence. Students actually learn more with a few bumps in the flow — reflection questions, “why did that fail?” moments, structured struggles. Smooth isn’t always better. Ask any whale.

### So what?

Biomimicry isn’t about “nature is nice, let’s hug trees.” It’s: Nature solved it first, and we should be humble enough to copy.

And in learning design, that means:

- Iteration without ego (nature doesn’t care if you like its UI).
- Systems that self-regulate instead of endless manual fixes.
- Micro-grips that make progress sticky instead of giant scary leaps.
- Designing turbulence that teaches resilience, not just smooth sailing.

### The final brain fart 💨

While we fetishise “disruption” and “innovation,” termites, geckos, and kingfishers just roll their eyes (if they had them). They already worked it out. Maybe our job in design — whether UX or learning — is less about being “creative geniuses” and more about being observant copycats.

After all, the kingfisher didn’t need a post-it wall to be aerodynamic.
author: Rich Bartlett
